draft.introduction: The Book of Jubilees is a Second Temple Jewish text, likely composed in Hebrew in the second century BCE. It retells Genesis and the first part of Exodus within a framework of angelic revelation: the Angel of the Presence dictates the entire history to Moses on Mount Sinai. The text survives complete only in Ethiopic, though Hebrew fragments were found among the Dead Sea Scrolls at Qumran, confirming its antiquity and its importance to at least some Jewish communities before the Common Era. Chapter 2 retells the creation account of Genesis 1, but with a crucial difference: God creates through angels. The angels of the presence, the angels of sanctification, and other angelic orders are themselves created on the first day and then participate in the rest of creation. This angelic framework transforms the Genesis narrative from a simple creation story into a cosmological hierarchy. For the theosis question, Jubilees matters because it establishes a model in which the boundary between divine and created beings is permeable—angels are created, yet they stand in God’s presence and participate in God’s creative work.

translation.text: And the angel of the presence spoke to Moses according to the word of the Lord, saying: “Write the complete history of the creation, how in six days the Lord God finished all his works and all that he created, and kept Sabbath on the seventh day and hallowed it for all ages.”

“For on the first day he created the heavens which are above, and the earth, and the waters and all the spirits who serve before him: the angels of the presence, and the angels of sanctification, and the angels of the spirit of fire, and the angels of the spirit of the winds.”

“And the angels of the spirit of the clouds, and of darkness, and of snow, and of hail, and of frost, and the angels of the voices and of thunder and of lightning, and the angels of the spirits of cold and of heat, and of winter and of spring and of autumn and of summer.”

“And all the spirits of his creatures which are in the heavens and on the earth: the abysses and the darkness, and evening and night, and the light and the dawn and the day, which he prepared in the knowledge of his heart. And then we saw his works, and praised him, and lauded before him on account of all his works; for seven great works he created on the first day.”

“On the second day he created the firmament in the midst of the waters, and the waters were divided on that day: half of them went up above the firmament and half of them went down below the firmament, in the midst over the face of the whole earth. And this was the only work he created on the second day.”

“On the third day he commanded the waters to pass from off the face of the whole earth into one place, and the dry land to appear. And the waters did so as he commanded them, and they retired from the face of the earth into one place beyond this firmament, and the dry land appeared.”

“On that day he created for the earth all the seeds according to their kinds and their species, and all the fruit-bearing trees and all trees of the forest. And on the fourth day he created the sun and the moon and the stars, and set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light upon all the earth.”

“And to rule over the day and the night, and to divide the light from the darkness. And God appointed the sun to be a great sign over the earth for days and for sabbaths and for months and for feasts and for years and for sabbaths of years and for jubilees and for all seasons of the years.”

translator_notes.note: The creation of angels on the first day is Jubilees’ most significant departure from Genesis. Genesis 1 mentions no angels; Jubilees places them at the very beginning, as part of the foundational structure of reality. The two highest orders—angels of the presence and angels of sanctification—are distinguished from the nature-angels who govern weather and seasons. This hierarchy would become enormously influential in later Jewish and Christian angelology.

The shift from third person to first person (“then we saw his works, and praised him”) reveals the narrator: the Angel of the Presence is speaking as a witness to creation. This makes the entire text an angelic testimony, adding a layer of authority that the Genesis account lacks. The seven great works on the first day (heavens, earth, waters, spirits, darkness, light, and the abyss) establish the Jubilees cosmology as more elaborate than the Genesis original.

The calendar is central to Jubilees’ theology. The sun, not the moon, governs the sacred calendar—a point of fierce controversy in Second Temple Judaism. The Qumran community followed a solar calendar, and the presence of Jubilees fragments at Qumran confirms this connection. The word “jubilees” in the title refers to the 49-year cycles that structure all of sacred history.
